content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 5,
1992
Charles H.
Bell, Jr.
BELL
&
HILTACHK
555 Capitol Mall,
Suite 530
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No.
1-92—288
Dear Mr.
Bell:
I
am writing to confirm our telephone conversation of May 29,
1992,
wherein we discussed your request for informal advice,
Letter No.
1-92-288.
You represent a campaign media consultant
who is seeking to obtain free time from a broadcast station to air
spot advertisements for one side of
a ballot measure.
You would
like to know if such free broadcast time is considered a
contribution.
Since your question is general in nature and you have not
named a specific public official or committee on whose behalf you
have requested advice,
we can only provide the following informal
response to your question.
1
The definition of contribution in Section 82015 includes:
the granting of discounts or rebates not
extended to the public generally or the granting of
discounts or rebates by television and radio
stations and newspapers not extended on an equal
basis to all candidates for the same office;...
I informed you that the orten Advice Letter,
No.
A-88-293
(copy enclosed)
applied this section and concluded that if the
radio station has a policy of providing free advertising time to
both sides of a campaign or a particular campaign issue,
the free
time is not a reportable contribution.
1
Informal assistance does nQt provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3) .)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916)322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-288
Page
2
The letter also stated in footnote
3 that it is not necessary
that the station actually provide free advertising time to each
candidate or side of an issue.
The intent of the above—quoted
provision of Section 82015 will be met as long as the station has
a policy of providing equal time to each candidate or to each
committee for both sides of a ballot measure.
Therefore,
the donation of free radio time to one side of a
ballot measure,
which is not offered to the other side of the
ballot measure,
is considered a contribution.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
H
—
By:
Jill Stecher
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:JS:aa
Enclosure

sections.question: You represent a campaign media consultant who is seeking to obtain free time from a broadcast station to air spot advertisements for one side of a ballot measure. You would like to know if such free broadcast time is considered a contribution.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, the donation of free radio time to one side of a ballot measure, which is not offered to the other side of the ballot measure, is considered a contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether free broadcast time granted by a station to air advertisements for one side of a ballot measure constitutes a reportable contribution under FPPC law.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free broadcast time is a contribution unless the station has a policy of providing equal free time to both sides of the ballot measure issue. If a station offers free time to only one side without offering equal time to the other side, it constitutes a reportable contribution.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter (provides limited immunity per Government Code Section 83114). References prior opinion letter A-88-293. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but content clearly delineates the inquiry and response. Document confirms telephone conversation from May 29, 1992.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You represent a campaign media consultant who is seeking to obtain free time from a broadcast station to air spot advertisements for one side of a ballot measure. You would like to know if such free broadcast time is considered a contribution.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, the donation of free radio time to one side of a ballot measure, which is not offered to the other side of the ballot measure, is considered a contribution.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 5, 1992 Charles H. Bell, Jr. BELL & HILTACHK 555 Capitol Mall, Suite 530 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Advice Our File No. 1-92—288 Dear Mr. Bell: I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation of May 29, 1992, wherein we discussed your request for informal advice, Letter No. 1-92-288. You represent a campaign media consultant who is seeking to obtain free time from a broadcast station to air spot advertisements for one side of a ballot measure. You would like to know if such free broadcast time is considered a contribution. Since your question is general in nature and you have not named a specific public official or committee on whose behalf you have requested advice, we can only provide the following informal response to your question. 1 The definition of contribution in Section 82015 includes: the granting of discounts or rebates not extended to the public generally or the granting of discounts or rebates by television and radio stations and newspapers not extended on an equal basis to all candidates for the same office;... I informed you that the orten Advice Letter, No. A-88-293 (copy enclosed) applied this section and concluded that if the radio station has a policy of providing free advertising time to both sides of a campaign or a particular campaign issue, the free time is not a reportable contribution. 1 Informal assistance does nQt provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c) (3) .) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. 1-92-288 Page 2 The letter also stated in footnote 3 that it is not necessary that the station actually provide free advertising time to each candidate or side of an issue. The intent of the above—quoted provision of Section 82015 will be met as long as the station has a policy of providing equal time to each candidate or to each committee for both sides of a ballot measure. Therefore, the donation of free radio time to one side of a ballot measure, which is not offered to the other side of the ballot measure, is considered a contribution. I trust this answers your question. Sincerely, Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel H — By: Jill Stecher Counsel, Legal Division SH:JS:aa Enclosure
embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter responding to a media consultant's question about whether free broadcast time for ballot measure advertisements constitutes a reportable contribution. The FPPC concluded that free time is a contribution unless the station has a policy of providing equal time to both sides of the measure.
